studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is teaching a client with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) about lifestyle modifications to take to reduce GERD symptoms. The nurse determines there is a need for further teaching if the client makes which statement?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Lifestyle modifications for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) include weight reduction if appropriate, decreasing caffeine and alcohol intake, avoiding lying down for at least 3 hours after eating, and eating small, frequent meals. Option 4 is the statement that requires further teaching because the client should eat small, frequent meals rather than one large meal, which would exacerbate symptoms. Therefore, option 4 is the client statement that presents a need for further teaching.

optionList.content: I plan to eat four to six small meals a day.
I need to sleep in the right side-lying position.
I plan to have a snack 1 hour before going to bed.
I will stop having a glass of wine each evening with dinner.
I plan to eat four to six small meals a day.
I need to sleep in the right side-lying position.
I plan to have a snack 1 hour before going to bed.
I will stop having a glass of wine each evening with dinner.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and provides client education on measures to decrease GERD. Which statement made by the client indicates a need for further teaching?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The control of GERD involves lifestyle changes to promote health and control reflux. These lifestyle changes include eating four to six small meals a day; avoiding alcohol and smoking; sleeping in the right side-lying position to promote oxygenation; and frequent swallowing to clear the esophagus; and avoiding eating at least 3 hours before going to bed because reflux episodes are most damaging at night.

optionList.content: “I will not feel pain with this scan.”
“I can have a light snack during the night or in the morning if I am hungry.”
“I am going to have to lie on my back for the scan.”
“I will have to wear an abdominal binder during the scan.”
“I will not feel pain with this scan.”
“I can have a light snack during the night or in the morning if I am hungry.”

“I will have to wear an abdominal binder during the scan.”

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse has been assigned a client who is going for a gastroesophageal (GE) reflux scan in the morning. Which statement by the client indicates a need for further teaching?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
A gastroesophageal (GE) reflux scan is a painless test used to help determine the speed in which the stomach empties for clients with symptoms of vomiting, heartburn, regurgitation, and dysphagia. The client is placed in supine position with an abdominal binder during a GE scan. The nurse would instruct the client not to eat or drink after midnight to prevent complications of aspiration.

optionList.content: “The fundus of the stomach is wrapped around the lower esophagus and then sutured in place.”
“A flexible scope is inserted down the esophagus and a balloon is inflated to dilate the esophagus.”
“A small, flexible ring of titanium magnets is placed laparoscopically into the lower esophageal sphincter.”
“Radiofrequency waves are delivered to the esophageal mucosa, which results in lesions that thicken the lower esophageal sphincter.”
“The fundus of the stomach is wrapped around the lower esophagus and then sutured in place.”
“A flexible scope is inserted down the esophagus and a balloon is inflated to dilate the esophagus.”
“A small, flexible ring of titanium magnets is placed laparoscopically into the lower esophageal sphincter.”
“Radiofrequency waves are delivered to the esophageal mucosa, which results in lesions that thicken the lower esophageal sphincter.”

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is reinforcing education to a client diagnosed with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) regarding surgical therapy for the condition. How would the nurse describe laparoscopic fundoplication?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Surgical therapy is reserved for clients with severe gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) despite maximal medical management. There are several procedures used for intractable GERD, including laparoscopic fundoplication, the insertion of a LINX reflux management system, and radiofrequency ablation. Eliminate option 2, as this describes esophageal dilation, which is used to treat esophageal strictures. Eliminate option 3, as this describes the insertion of a LINX system. Eliminate option 4, as this describes radiofrequency ablation. Laparoscopic fundoplication refers to wrapping the fundus of the stomach around the lower esophagus and suturing it in place. Therefore, option 1 is correct.

optionList.content: Burp the infant less frequently during feedings.
Thin the feedings by adding water to the formula.
Thicken the feedings by adding rice cereal to the formula.
Burp the infant less frequently during feedings.
Thin the feedings by adding water to the formula.
Thicken the feedings by adding rice cereal to the formula.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse provides feeding instructions to the parent of an infant diagnosed with gastroesophageal reflux disease. Which instruction would the nurse give to the parent to assist in reducing the episodes of emesis?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Gastroesophageal reflux is backflow of gastric contents into the esophagus as a result of relaxation or incompetence of the lower esophageal or cardiac sphincter. Small, more frequent feedings with frequent burping often are prescribed in the treatment of gastroesophageal reflux. Feedings thickened with rice cereal may reduce episodes of emesis. If thickened formula is used, cross-cutting of the nipple may be required.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) has been assigned to the nurse for the day. The nurse plans to teach the client that which foods contribute to decreased lower esophageal sphincter pressure? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Consuming caffeinated beverages (cola and coffee), chocolate, and peppermint leads to reflux and irritation of the esophagus through decreased lower esophageal sphincter pressure. When the lower esophageal sphincter pressure is decreased, gastric contents reflux into the esophagus and cause a client to have GERD. Options 3 and 6 do not contribute to decreased pressure.

optionList.content: Eat four to six small meals a day.
Keep the head of the bed elevated at 60 degrees.
The importance of remaining supine for 2 to 3 hours after a meal.
The importance of wearing tight, restrictive clothing around the waist.
Eat four to six small meals a day.
Keep the head of the bed elevated at 60 degrees.
The importance of remaining supine for 2 to 3 hours after a meal.
The importance of wearing tight, restrictive clothing around the waist.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client is newly diagnosed with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Which lifestyle modifications would be most important for the nurse to encourage when educating the client? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Nursing care priorities for a client with GERD focus on lifestyle modifications that are used to improve comfort and care. Smoking can lead to immediate decrease in pressure in the lower esophageal sphincter. Gastric contents reflux when pressure in the lower esophageal sphincter is decreased, so smoking cessation would be encouraged. Eating four to six small meals each day rather than three large meals will decrease volume and pressure in the stomach and aid in gastric emptying. Clients who are overweight or obese have increased intra-abdominal pressure, which contributes to gastroesophageal reflux. The head of the bed would be elevated to 30 degrees, and the client would not wear tight, restrictive clothing around the waist; elevating the head to 60 degrees and wearing tight clothing can increase intra-abdominal pressure and can further trigger symptoms of GERD. Thus, options 3 and 6 would be eliminated. The client would not lie supine for 2 to 3 hours after a meal, as sitting upright for at least one hour after eating can promote proper digestion and reduce the risk of reflux. Therefore, eliminate option 5.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is providing dietary education to a client with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). The nurse indicates that the client understands the teaching if the client states a plan to avoid which foods to prevent symptom exacerbation? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) results when gastric contents regurgitate through the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) and into the esophagus. This acid can contain both gastric and intestinal proteolytic enzymes, which are even more irritating to the esophageal mucosa. A big component of managing GERD is to modify the diet and avoid certain foods. These foods include tea, beer, coffee, and chocolate. Other foods to avoid include fatty foods and peppermint or spearmint. Eliminate options 4 and 6, as these are high-fiber foods that may help GERD symptoms. Therefore, if the client states an intention to avoid options 1, 2, 3, and 5, then the nurse can deduce that the client understands the dietary teaching.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is teaching dietary modifications to a client diagnosed with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Which would the nurse recommend to prevent exacerbation of GERD symptoms? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Dietary modification is an important part of GERD management. High-fiber foods and foods with a high water content are useful in lessening the severity of GERD symptoms. Foods to avoid include carbonated beverages and high-fat foods, such as whole milk. Therefore, eliminate options 3 and 5. Options 1, 2, and 4 are the correct answers.

optionList.content: Reflux of bile from the small intestine
Reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus
Reflux of bile from the small intestine
Reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus

studentQuestionArray.content: A client asks the nurse what causes gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Knowing that GERD has many causative factors, the nurse would list which as contributors to GERD? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is a multifactorial disorder that occurs when intestinal and gastric contents move up into the esophagus, ultimately irritating and damaging the esophageal mucosa. Eliminate option 1 because delayed gastric emptying, not rapid gastric emptying, contributes to GERD. Options 2, 3, 4, and 5 are all factors that contribute to GERD. Therefore, options 2, 3, 4 and 5 are correct.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is planning to teach a client with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) about substances to avoid. Which items would the nurse include on this list? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Foods that decrease lower esophageal sphincter (LES) pressure and irritate the esophagus will increase reflux and exacerbate the symptoms of GERD and therefore need to be avoided. Aggravating substances include coffee, chocolate, peppermint, fried or fatty foods, carbonated beverages, and alcohol. Options 4 and 6 do not promote this effect.
